title: CHP in Freefall, NATO Summit Repression Escalates, Halkbank Walks Free
subhead: Turkey's main opposition implodes between duelling leaders and judicial intervention, while Ankara cracks down on dissent ahead of the July NATO summit and a US judge dismisses the Halkbank indictment — giving Erdogan a clean slate for his hosting role.

sections.heading: CHP Crisis Deepens: Duelling Leaders, Resignations, Evictions
sections.body:
Turkey's main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) is in open disarray after a court ruling last month annulled the 2023 party congress that elected Ozgur Ozel as chairman, reinstating former leader Kemal Kilicdaroglu. The crisis escalated on June 9 when both leaders held simultaneous rival meetings: Ozel told CHP lawmakers in parliament that 'the point is not to give up, not to surrender, but to resist,' while Kilicdaroglu told supporters at party headquarters he would 'cleanse the party of dirt' (Reuters, US News).

On June 11, Ozel's entire team resigned from the CHP party assembly, a move his office said should legally trigger an extraordinary congress to re-elect a chairman (Reuters). Earlier, on May 24, Turkish riot police fired tear gas and forced their way into CHP headquarters to evict Ozel's ousted leadership (Reuters).

Two-thirds of CHP's 138 MPs voted to keep Ozel as parliamentary group leader after the court ruling, and party supporters on Büyükada staged mass protests Sunday over the arrest of Adalar (Büyükada) Mayor Ercan Akpolat — one of 47 detention orders targeting CHP-linked officials, 42 of whom were taken into custody (Al Monitor, MSN/Reuters). The conventional wisdom among observers is that after the July 7-8 NATO summit, Ozel's parliamentary immunity may be stripped and he could face imprisonment (Al Monitor).
sections.whyItMatters: The CHP is running roughly even with Erdogan's AKP in opinion polls. If the party splits or is neutralized through judicial pressure, the path clears for Erdogan to extend his 23-year rule beyond the scheduled 2028 election — or call an early vote while the opposition is shattered.

sections.heading: NATO Summit Repression: Arrests, Website Bans, Public Event Freeze
sections.body:
Ankara is imposing a de facto state of emergency ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit. According to monitoring site Engelli Web, online reports and activism websites critical of the summit have been blocked in Turkey (Yahoo/Engelli Web). Students in Ankara are protesting planned security restrictions (Yahoo News).

More concretely, a wave of preventive arrests has targeted anti-war and anti-NATO activists. On June 12, 14 students from the Revolutionary Youth Associations (DGD) were detained in simultaneous raids across eight provinces; 10 were subsequently jailed. Their alleged crime: 'activities carried out against NATO and imperialism.' On June 15, 23 people were detained from trade unions and leftist organizations in Istanbul; 21 were arrested on June 18, some without even having their statements taken by prosecutors (WSWS).

From July 6-12, all public events in Ankara are banned — including exams, concerts, and graduation ceremonies. The nationwide teaching exam has been postponed to July 26. Some 70,000 security personnel (55,000 police and gendarmerie) will be deployed for roughly 6,000 summit participants (WSWS, citing TRT).
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is using the NATO summit to blanket domestic repression with a security rationale. The scale of preventive detentions and event bans is unprecedented for a NATO gathering and signals how far the government will go to suppress visible dissent while the world's cameras are in town.

sections.heading: Halkbank Case Dismissed: US Judge Ends Iran Sanctions Prosecution
sections.body:
A US federal judge on June 17 formally dismissed the criminal indictment against Turkey's state-owned Halkbank, ending a prosecution that began in 2019 over the bank's role in a $20 billion Iran sanctions evasion scheme. The dismissal came after the Trump administration's Justice Department reached a deferred prosecution agreement with Halkbank (Reuters, CryptoBriefing, MSN).

The original indictment accused Halkbank of secretly transferring restricted Iranian funds, converting oil revenue into gold and cash, and using fake food shipments as cover. The judge weighed and approved the deal on Wednesday, formally closing the case (Reuters).

Al Monitor notes this is a huge win for Erdogan, removing a major irritant in US-Turkey relations just weeks before he hosts the NATO summit in Ankara.
sections.whyItMatters: The Halkbank dismissal removes the last major legal obstacle in US-Turkey bilateral relations ahead of the NATO summit. It also signals that the Trump administration is willing to drop sanctions enforcement cases to smooth relations with strategic partners — a precedent that will not go unnoticed in other capitals.

sections.heading: Hormuz Bypass Corridors: Syria and Turkey as Post-Strait Energy Hubs
sections.body:
Even with a US-Iran ceasefire agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, regional states are doubling down on overland alternatives. The biggest new framework is the Four Seas Initiative, launched June 11 by the Washington-based New Lines Institute with US congressional and diplomatic support. It proposes $10 billion in infrastructure to build pipelines through Syria and Turkey capable of transporting up to 4 million barrels of oil per day and 50 bcm/year of gas to Europe — enough to reduce Hormuz dependency significantly (OilPrice, New Lines Institute).

On the ground, Syria is already functioning as a bypass. The port of Baniyas — largely inactive for over a decade — is now handling 90,000 barrels of Iraqi oil per day, with 1,400 trucks daily crossing from Iraq through Syria. Cranes are dredging the harbor to accept larger vessels (CS Monitor).

Turkey is positioning itself as the northern anchor. Energy Minister Alparslan Bayraktar announced enhanced gas supply contracts with Gazprom at the Baku Energy Forum on May 31, while simultaneously pushing Turkey as Europe's energy hub (JPost, Bloomberg). Separately, Turkey and NATO are working on a $28 billion pipeline expansion connecting underground fuel infrastructure to bolster alliance fuel security (Bloomberg).
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran war has accelerated what was already a strategic trend: the diversification of energy routes away from Hormuz. Syria's new government under al-Sharaa is the unexpected beneficiary, while Turkey gains leverage over both European energy security and Gulf states seeking overland export paths.

sections.heading: CBRT Holds Rate at 37% for Third Meeting
sections.body:
Turkey's central bank left its one-week repo rate at 37% on June 11, the third consecutive hold. The monetary policy committee cited elevated energy prices due to the Middle East conflict and continued inflationary pressures as reasons for caution (Reuters, MSN, WSJ).

Official CPI inflation was 32.61% year-over-year in May, edging up from 32.37% in April (TurkStat). Koc University economist Selva Demiralp noted a 'significant relationship' between political preferences and central bank rate decisions in Turkey, arguing the CBRT's hands are 'tied' — with a possible early election creating an easing bias despite weak economic justification (CNBC).
sections.whyItMatters: The central bank is in a box: inflation remains above 30%, the Iran war keeps energy costs elevated, and political pressure to ease ahead of possible early elections conflicts with price stability. The 37% hold is a holding pattern that reflects paralysis more than strategy.

sections.heading: Defense Portfolio Grows: IHA-230 Enters Service, Bangladesh Expands Partnership
sections.body:
Turkey's defense export push continues on multiple tracks. The IHA-230, a UAV-launched ballistic supersonic missile with a range exceeding 150 km, has entered service with the Turkish Land Forces, announced by Ministry of National Defense spokesman Rear Admiral Zeki Akturk (Janes, MSN).

Bangladesh-Turkiye defense cooperation is advancing from purchases to joint production. Foreign Minister Hakan Fidan's three-day visit to Dhaka in early June established ministerial-level joint committees on defense and foreign affairs and annual '2+2' consultations. Bangladesh has already deployed 12 Bayraktar TB2 drones and at least 18 Roketsan TRG-300 Kaplan rocket launchers, and Dhaka is now interested in producing drones domestically with Turkish assistance. The two countries aim to lift bilateral trade to $2 billion (The Diplomat).

More broadly, Turkey now supplies weapons to nearly 40 countries and is pushing for deeper integration into Western defense supply chains as Europe rearms (Reuters, MSN).
sections.whyItMatters: Turkey's defense industry is shifting from export customer to co-production partner — the Bangladesh model of joint manufacturing could replicate across South and Southeast Asia. The IHA-230's entry into service also signals Turkey's move beyond platforms (drones) into precision munitions that extend strike range.

bottomLine: Three forces converged this week: the systematic dismantling of Turkey's main opposition through judicial intervention, a sweeping domestic security crackdown ahead of the July 7-8 NATO summit, and the removal of the Halkbank legal obstacle in US-Turkey relations. Erdogan enters his hosting role with a weakened opposition at home, a muzzled protest environment, and a clean bilateral slate with Washington. Meanwhile, the Hormuz crisis is reshaping Turkey's strategic value — as Syria and Turkey emerge as overland energy corridors, Ankara gains leverage it didn't have before the Iran war. The central bank's rate hold at 37% reflects not policy confidence but political constraint: with early elections possible and inflation above 30%, easing risks a lira crisis while holding chokes growth.
sources: Reuters, Al Monitor, WSWS, MSN, US News, CryptoBriefing, OilPrice, CSMonitor, Janes, The Diplomat, Bloomberg, WSJ, CNBC, Yahoo/EngelliWeb, JPost, Bianet, Daily Sabah, Anadolu Agency, New Lines Institute
